coredata.dc:title: Acid treatment to tune the optical properties of carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Oh, Geun-Hyeong
Kim, Bo-Seok
Song, Youngjun
Kim, Sejung
coredata.dc:description: The photophysical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be manipulated by doping external elements to control photoluminescence (PL) emission wavelength and enhance quantum yield during either growth or post-treatment. However, the utilization of CQDs has been limited by broad bandwidth of emission and the requirements of a multi-step purification processes. Here, we report an efficient process – a simple acid treatment (H2SO4 or HCl) - for producing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) capable of multicolor emission. Using this acid treatment, the full-width half maximum (FWHM) of the resulting NCQDs PL emissions can be narrowed by approximately 46 nm in the visible light region. We also determined that the crystallinity of the NCQDs can be enhanced without any significant changes in the density of oxygen-included functional groups, and the lifetime of the charge carriers falls within a range of 3.36 and 6.27 ns. We anticipate that our approach will allow for the practical utilization of NCQDs in a variety of optoelectronic devices in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Acid treatment
Bandgap
Full-width half maximum (FWHM)
Multicolor emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Acid treatment of blue-light emissive NCQDs
Acid treatment of multicolor emission of NCQDs
Graphitic structures and elemental analysis of NCQDs
Measurement of time-resolved photoluminescence of NCQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Multicolor emissive NCQDs. (a) schematic diagram of NCQD formation, (b) multicolor emission of NCQDs under UV irradiation at 365 nm, (c-f) TEM images of the NCQDs (scale bar: 5 nm), (g-j) size distributions of the NCQDs.

Optical properties of blue emissive NCQDs. (a) PL and PLE of NCQDs in DI water, (b) excitation-dependent PL of NCQDs in DI water, (c) Tauc plot of NCQDs in DI water, (d) PL and PLE of NCQDs in 1 M of H2SO4, (e) excitation-dependent PL of NCQDs in 1 M of H2SO4, (f) Tauc plot of NCQDs in 1 M of H2SO4, (g) PL and PLE of NCQDs in 3 M of H2SO4, (h) excitation-dependent PL of NCQDs in 3 M of H2SO4, (i) Tauc plot of NCQDs in 3 M of H2SO4. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Optical properties of cyan- and red-emissive NCQDs. (a) PL and PLE, (b) excitation-dependent PL, (c) Tauc plot of NCQDs in DMF, (d) PL and PLE of HCl treated NCQDs, (e) excitation-dependent PL of HCl treated NCQDs, (f) Tauc plot of HCl treated NCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Raman spectroscopy of acid treated NCQDs. (a) blue-light emissive NCQDs, (b) 1 M of H2SO4 treated NCQDs, (c) 3 M of H2SO4 treated NCQDs, (d) HCl treated NCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). (a) blue-light emissive NCQDs, (b) 1 M of H2SO4 treated NCQDs, (c) 3 M of H2SO4 treated NCQDs, (d) HCl treated NCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Assessment of NCQD lifetime. (a) blue-light emissive NCQDs, (b) 1 M of H2SO4 treated NCQDs, (c) 3 M of H2SO4 treated NCQDs, (d) HCl treated NCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Acid treatment to tune the optical properties of carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Clean Energy Research Center, Jeonbuk National University, 567 Baekjedae-ro, Jeonju-si, Jeollabuk-do 54896, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Clean Energy Research Center, Jeonbuk National University, 567 Baekjedae-ro, Jeonju-si, Jeollabuk-do 54896, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nano-bioengineering, Incheon National University, Academy-to 119, Incheon 22012, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nano-bioengineering, Incheon National University, Academy-to 119, Incheon 22012, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photophysical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be manipulated by doping external elements to control photoluminescence (PL) emission wavelength and enhance quantum yield during either growth or post-treatment. However, the utilization of CQDs has been limited by broad bandwidth of emission and the requirements of a multi-step purification processes. Here, we report an efficient process – a simple acid treatment (H2SO4 or HCl) - for producing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) capable of multicolor emission. Using this acid treatment, the full-width half maximum (FWHM) of the resulting NCQDs PL emissions can be narrowed by approximately 46 nm in the visible light region. We also determined that the crystallinity of the NCQDs can be enhanced without any significant changes in the density of oxygen-included functional groups, and the lifetime of the charge carriers falls within a range of 3.36 and 6.27 ns. We anticipate that our approach will allow for the practical utilization of NCQDs in a variety of optoelectronic devices in the near future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional quantum dots (QDs), including heavy metals such as cadmium included QDs, have high quantum yields (>90%) with narrow full-width half maximum (FWMH) of approximately 35 nm, as well as a size dependent controllable bandgap [1–4]. Although QDs are critical components of optoelectronic, bioimaging, and photovoltaic devices for high performance, the amount of heavy metals that can be employed in nanomaterials is limited by both their intrinsic toxicity and the associated Restriction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS) promulgated by the European Union [5–7]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a controllable bandgap, which consist of sp2 - and sp3 -carbon bonding structures with a few nanometers in diameter, have drawn attentions of researchers for their superior optical properties, physical stability, low toxicity, and environmentally-friendly nature [8–10]. Due to the presence of functional groups (e.g. –COOH, –OH, and –NH2) on the surface of CQDs, the introduction of aliphatic or stimuli-responsive carbon chains into CQDs allows them to form high-performance photocatalysts, high-brightness light-emitting devices, and multi-colored bioimaging [11–14].

The colors emitted by traditional QDs are dependent on their sizes according to the quantum confinement effects (QCEs) [2,17]. However, the mechanism of light emissions from CQDs, either QCEs or specific structure dependent emission, still remains unknown [2,17,24–27]. Typically, the specific structure-associated phenomena, including self-localized charges and surface defect-trapped carriers, are involved, resulting in the broadband fluorescence spectra observed in CQDs. For self-localized charges, photogenerated charge carriers are stabilized by strong electron-phonon coupling, resulting in large-amplitude vibrations and distortions. The surface defect-trapped carriers usually result from electron-withdrawing oxygen-containing groups, including carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups on the surface of the CQDs [24–30]. With respect to CQDs, non-radiative excited-state relaxation process should be restricted to enhance their optical properties.

Due to their light absorption capabilities, CQDs can be used as photocatalysts to remove organic pollutants under the light irradiation. When they absorb light in the UV–vis range and visible light range, CQDs respectively induce the π-π* transition of sp2 -carbon bonding in the core and the n-π* transition in outer layer [31,32]. The electrons move from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to their lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), resulting a hole in the HOMO. These in turn transform dissolved oxygen and OH ions into their reactive species (
O2 – and
OH radicals), which are capable of degrading organic pollutants [33]. Unfortunately, absorption in the range of UV light is not beneficial to those working in the field of photocatalysis, as solar energy is largely concentrated in the visible light region. Recently, it was shown that the introduction of nitrogen into CQDs achieves absorption in the NIR-I regions by forming pyrrolic nitrogen on the surfaces [34]. As an alternative, CQDs can be integrated with narrow bandgap semiconductors such as TiO2, WO3, and g-C3N4, which are able to absorb light in the broad region to visible light region [35–37].

CQDs with controllable bandgaps can easily be produced by the mixture of sp2 - and sp3 -carbon bonds through hydro- and solvothermal processes, which enhance quantum yields, and improve surface functionalities [19–23]. The fragmentations of carbon structures (i.e. graphene and its derivatives) into a few nanometers through electrochemical and ball-milling methods are limited to adjust CQDs bandgap, and associated with poor production efficiency and quantum yields [15–18]. For this reason, CQDs with desirable optical properties can be mass produced by selecting appropriate precursors and synthetic processes.

Herein, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) were synthesized with precursors such as citric acid and urea as carbon sources and nitrogen sources in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) at 200 °C to obtain multicolor emissive CQDs with a narrow FWHM. The produced NCQDs were subsequently treated with diluted strong acids, such as hydrochloric or sulfuric acid, to investigate the effect of acid treatment on their bandgap, FWHM, and the lifetime of charge carriers.

Citric acid, urea, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and hydrazine hydrate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethyl alcohol, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35.0–37.0%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95%), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, bead) were purchased from Samchun chemical. Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Ammonia solution (NH3OH) was purchased from JUNSEI. All studies utilized deionized water.

NCQDs were prepared using the same molality of urea (10 mmol) and citric acid (10 mmol) in 10 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, where it was maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. The NCQDs were collected by centrifuging the reaction solution at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the solid material. The supernatant solutions were added to ethyl acetate, isopropyl alcohol, and acetone to obtain blue, green, and red emissive NCQDs, respectively. The obtained NCQDs were then re-dispersed in ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acid treatment of blue-light emissive NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: NCQDs were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment. Briefly, citric acid (1 mol) and urea (1 mol) were dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water or diluted sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 1 M or 3 M). The reactant solution was shifted into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The product solutions from the autoclave were then naturally cooled down to room temperature. The acidic solution was neutralized by sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) followed by dialysis for a week.

Citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were graphitized in DMF at 160 °C for 6 h and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. The obtained dark brown solutions were mixed with 5 mL of NaOH aqueous solution (50 mg mL−1) and 10 mL of ethanol, followed by sonication and centrifugation at 13500 rpm for 10 min. The precipitates were collected and dissolved in DI water followed by centrifugation at 13500 rpm for 10 min twice to wash off residual salts. Dark brown powdered NCQDs were obtained by freeze drying the precipitates for 48 h. Subsequently, the obtained dark powders were dissolved in 5 mL of dilute HCl aqueous solution (5 wt%) and DI water (15 mL), then sonicated for 10 min to remove passivated metal cations on the surface of NCQDs. The solution was centrifuged at 13500 rpm for 10 min and the precipitates were collected, then dissolved in water and centrifuged (13500 rpm, 10 min) twice to remove residual salts and HCl. The product was then collected by freeze drying for 48 h.

Ultraviolet–visible absorption and PL emission spectra were recorded with a Jasco V-670 UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer and a Jasco FP-6500 spectrofluorometer, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum was measured with a Jasco FT/IR-4100 Infrared spectroscope installed in the Future Energy Convergence Core Center (FECC) at Jeonbuk National University. Raman spectra were measured with a Nanophoton Raman spectrometer installed in the Center for University-wide Research Facilities (CURF) at Jeonbuk National University at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured with an NEXSA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer system. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were measured with a HORIBA Fluorolog3 TCSPC spectrofluorometer at an excitations 374 nm and 467 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained with a FEI Titan G2 Cube 60-300 transmission electron microscope at 80 kV.

Fig. 1 a demonstrates a simple process for producing multicolor emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) by the graphitization of citric acid and urea (carbon source and nitrogen source) with the same molar ratio in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) at 200 °C in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The NCQDs were collected by centrifuging the product solution at 10000 rpm for 15 min and purified through column chromatography. Fig. 1b shows multicolor emissive NCQDs in ethanol under irradiation of UV light at 365 nm. The resulting diameters of the NCQDs were obtained by analysis of TEM images: 3.96 ± 0.54 nm (blue), 4.12 ± 0.68 nm (green), 4.34 ± 0.49 nm (yellow), and 4.41 ± 0.51 nm (red) (Fig. 1c-j). Fig. 1c inset shows a representative high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the multicolor emissive NCQDs, highlighting their highly crystalline structures and 0.22 nm of lattice spacing, which are the result of the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes [14,38]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra show the maximum emission wavelength at 440 nm (blue), 545 nm (green), 565 nm (yellow), and 635 nm (red) ( Fig. S1 ).

As shown in Fig. S1
, although NCQDs were successfully separated into different emission colors (red, green, blue and yellow) as they were irradiated with UV light at 365 nm, the features of the PL emission spectra reveal a full-width half maximum (FWHM) with approximately 100 nm even after repeated column chromatography. Accordingly, although the purification steps involving column chromatography may be useful to obtain high-purity NCQDs, the process can only produce small quantities of NCQDs with a distinctive emission colors and 100 nm of FWHM. The specific structure-associated PL emission mechanism may provide broadband PL emission, as a result of the electron-phonon coupling and surface defects associated with the electron donating groups [13,17,26–28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acid treatment of multicolor emission of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The controllable PL properties of NCQDs can be achieved by adjusting the types of precursors used, the chemical treatment employed, and the solvent mixtures incorporated. If chosen well, PL quantum yields (QY) will be enhanced and a controllable bandgap of NCQDs for multicolor emission will be achieved [21,39–41]. In our synthetic processes, urea and citric acids were used as precursors, while a chemical treatment with diluted strong acids was used to investigate the effect of acid treatment on the optical properties of NCQDs.

Blue-light emissive NCQDs were prepared in aqueous acidic solutions with diverse concentrations of sulfuric acid (H2SO4); pure DI water (0 M), 1 M of H2SO4, and 3 M of H2SO4. For the synthesis of NCQDs in acidic solutions (1 and 3 M of H2SO4), the obtained dark brown solutions were neutralized by using sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), followed by a week of dialysis to remove salts. In Fig. S2
a-c, the absorbance spectrum exhibits a clear hypsochromic shift from 327 nm to 300 nm when either 1 or 3 M of H2SO4 were used as solvents. The PL emission of the NCQDs was also investigated (Fig. 2 ), and the maximum wavelength was determined to be 450 nm for DI water and 3 M of H2SO4. PL excitation (PLE) was shown at 410 nm (DI water) and 450 nm for DI water and 3 M (H2SO4), while in the case of 1 M of H2SO4, the maximum wavelength of PL and PLE were observed at 310 nm and 377 nm, respectively. The full-width half maximum (FWHM) of the PL emission was determined to be 46.8 nm when 1 M of H2SO4 was used ( Fig. S2
d-f). The excitation-dependent PL emissions of the NCQDs are shown in Fig. 2b, e, and h, which highlights their highly ordered graphitic structure [40]. Based on the absorbance spectrum ( Fig. S2
a-c), the bandgap of the NCQDs was calculated from Tauc plots as a function of (αhν)2 vs. energy. The bandgap (Eg) was 3.35 eV (DI water), 3.31 eV (1 M H2SO4), and 3.34 eV (3 M H2SO4) (Fig. 2c, f, i).

The cyan- and red-light emissive NCQDs were then treated with basic and acidic solutions; 1.25 M of NaOH was first applied, followed by 1.40 M of HCl solution. In Fig. S3
a, the absorbance peaks at 420 nm are attributed to the π- π* transitions of the C=C and C=N bonds in the aromatic rings, while the additional absorbance peak at 560 nm is attributed to the n– π* transition of the C=O bond [13,17,32,40]. The PL spectra of the NCQDs reflected a maximum wavelength at 475 nm (cyan-light emission) and 630 nm (red-light emission) (Fig. 3 ). Additionally, the PLE of the NCQDs was shown at 420 nm (cyan) and 560 nm (red). Using Beer-Lambert’s law, the absorption coefficient was calculated to be 480.611 mL mg−1 m−1 for various concentrations of NCQDs, as shown in Fig. S3
b [42]. Once the acid treatment was performed, the full-width half maximum (FWHM) of the NCQDs narrowed to ∼ 62 nm from 70 nm ( Fig. S3
c-d). In Fig. 3c and f, the bandgap of the NCQDs was calculated using a Tauc plot as a function of (αhν)2 vs. energy, yielding 1.87 eV and 2.09 eV for cyan- and red-light emissions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphitic structures and elemental analysis of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical treatment with acidic and basic solutions induced a peak shift of absorbance and PL emission, just as it narrowed FWHM by approximately 46 nm. To determine the mechanism responsible for this change in optical properties, the physicochemical properties of the NCQDs were investigated using Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

Raman spectroscopy was used to compare the disordered (D-band) and graphitic (G-band) structures of the NCQDs [25,29,43]. Two distinctive peaks are visible in Fig. 4 a-c; the D-bands are 1371, 1383, and 1372 cm−1, while the G-bands are 1581, 1580, and 1586 cm−1, for 0, 1, and 3 M of H2SO4, respectively. Those D- and G-bands correspond to the sp3 - and sp2 -carbon bonded structures. The intensity ratio of IG/ID is characteristic of the effective conjugation length of NCQDs; the ratio of IG/ID is 1.04, 1.13, and 1.12 for blue-light emissive NCQDs treated with 0 M, 1 M, and 3 M of H2SO4, respectively. In Fig. 4d, cyan- and red-light emissive NCDs were also investigated, in which the IG/ID intensity ratio (D-band at 1351 cm−1 and G-band at 1574 cm−1) is 0.87. This increase in the IG/ID suggests that these NCQDs possessed larger effective conjugation lengths than untreated NCQDs, while the high degree of crystallinity of the CQDs implies that the acid treatment of NCQDs induced greater NCQD graphitization [44].

X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the chemical compositions and surface states of the NCQDs. The survey spectrums ( Fig. S4 ) revealed that they are composed of carbon (C 1s), oxygen (O 1s), and nitrogen (N 1s). In Fig. 5 , the deconvolution of high resolution of C 1s XPS spectra for the NCQDs demonstrate three types of carbon bonding structures for acid treatments with various concentrations (0, 1, and 3 M H2SO4). This result shows that NCQDs treated with higher concentrations of H2SO4 possess more oxygen-included functional groups, a result similar to that obtained when strong acids are used to oxidize CQDs [40]. Additionally, high resolution XPS spectrum of the C 1s in the HCl treated NCQDs also revealed three different binding energy peaks at 284.6, 285.9 and 287.6 eV. These peaks suggest the presence of sp2 -carbon, C-N/C-O and C=O bonds, respectively (see Fig. 5).

In short, XPS analysis showed that NCQDs consist of sp2 -carbon bonds in the core and sp3 -carbon bonds, including carboxyl and amine groups, on the surface. In Fig. S4
d (high resolution image of N 1s spectrum), the deconvolution of N 1s suggests that nitrogen is chemically bonded into pyridinic N (399.5 eV), pyrrolic N (400.4 eV), and graphitic N (401.0 eV). In addition, we compared a diluted H2SO4 with a diluted HCl during the formation of NCQDs. When a diluted H2SO4 was used as a solvent, the formation of sp2 -carbon bonding (Fig. 4) and higher degrees of oxidation of NCQDs (Fig. 5b and c) were accelerated. A diluted HCl induced the increase in the amount of doped nitrogen ( Fig. S4 ), resulting in red-shifted PL emission (Fig. 3) [47]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were also performed to identify functional groups on the surface of the NCQDs ( Fig. S5 ) [19]. The broad absorption bands at 3100–3500 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H in hydroxyl and amine groups. The absorption band at 1189 cm−1 and 1715 cm−1 are assigned to C-O and C=O, respectively, which indicates that NCQDs have carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups on their surface [40,41].

Although the effective conjugation length is increased from blue, green, and red emissive NCQDs, they possess similar particle sizes in the range of 3 and 4 nm. In the case of our NCQDs, the surface states were the dominant factor by which we manipulated the PL spectrum: the luminescent centers on the surface of the NCQDs are predominantly composed of conjugated sp2 -carbon bonds and oxygen-included functional groups. The PL mechanisms of NCQDs can be exploited by either π-conjugation domains or the surface states of the NCQDs [24–29]. The former mechanism is based on the quantum size effect, in which a growing red-shifted emission wavelength results from a larger effective π-conjugation length and the density of delocalized electrons. As the Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 4) revealed, the π-conjugation length was increased by acid treatment, which we confirmed by comparing the IG/ID ratio. For acid treatment that employed a diluted H2SO4, the catalytic effects of H2SO4 increased the graphitization degrees of the NCQDs, a consequence of the acceleration of the hydration and carbonization reactions [45,46]. It is also supported by the PL emission dependency on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 and Fig. 3). Additionally, the PL analysis confirmed that an increase in the concentration of H2SO4 induces the red-shifted NCQD PL emissions. These surface defects are primarily created through surface oxidation; NCQDs with more oxidation may possess more surface defects, which corresponded with the presence of red-light emitting NCQDs produced by concentrated H2SO4 solution [48]. For the cyan- and red-light emitting NCQDs, their energy bandgap and FWHM in narrower after acid treatment, a consequence of the presence of carboxylic acid on sp2 -carbon structures which induces local distortions that serve as capture sites for charge carriers [19,24–28]. In previous reports, the density functional theory calculations revealed that a narrowed FWHM in the NCQDs was ascribed to delocalized wave-functions and smaller bandgap fluctuations, which prevented electronic coupling to molecular vibrations and distortions of the basal plane [28]. Likewise, when a diluted H2SO4 was employed in our synthetic process, an XPS analysis of the NCQDs revealed that the number of oxygen-contained functional groups (e.g. –OH, –COOH) had decreased (Fig. 5), and that the degrees of graphitization of the NCQDs had increased (Fig. 4). Therefore, we conclude that the narrower FWHM of the NCQDs is attributable to a higher degree of graphitization and lower number of oxygen-included functional groups, which results in delocalized wavefunctions and smaller bandgap fluctuation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of time-resolved photoluminescence of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We measured the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL), i.e., the time fluorophore resides in the excited state before giving a photon and turning back to the ground state, of the NCQDs. As shown in Fig. 6 (inserted), the average lifetime of NCQDs that received no acid treatment was 6.27 ns with the emission of 440 nm at the excitation wavelength of 316 nm. A reduced lifetime was observed in blue-light emissive NCQDs treated with diluted H2SO4; 3.36 ns for NCQDs treated with 1 M H2SO4 and 3.74 ns for NCQDs treated with 3 M H2SO4. This indicates that the longer lifetimes are associated with the presence of oxygen-included functional groups on the NCQDs [40].

The photoluminescence lifetime of the NCQDs treated with H2SO4 is increased from 3.36 ns (NCQDs treated with 1 M H2SO4) to 3.74 ns (NCQDs treated with 3 M H2SO4), again suggesting that the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers in the NCQDs are significantly affected by the density of the oxygen-included functional groups [40]. The TRPL spectra of the HCl-treated NCQDs showed that the average lifetime of the NCQDs was 4.57 ns at an emission wavelength of 460 nm when excitation wavelength of 374 nm was used. In sum, NCQDs with a higher degree of oxidation reflect a longer lifetime of the charge carriers as well as red-shifted emission wavelength.

In conclusion, multicolor emissive NCQDs with a narrow FWHM were synthesized using diluted strong acids such as H2SO4 and HCl. The optical properties of the resulting NCQDs can be changed by adjusting the density of the oxygen-included functional groups on the surface, resulting in a narrowing of the FWHM by approximately 42 nm and greater excitation-dependency PL emission. When it comes to comparison of 1 M and 3 M H2SO4 treated NCQDs, the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers was also increased by between from 3.36 and 3.74 ns. This increase was associated with an increase in the amount of oxygen-included functional groups present due to the serve as trap sites. We observed that the crystallinity of the NCQDs exerted a significant effect on the optical properties, which results from the comparison of IG/ID ratio for acid treated and untreated NCQDs. In light of the urgent need for multicolor emissive NCQDs with high quantum yields that can be produced at scale, this approach to the manipulation of optical properties via acid treatment allows for their incorporation into optoelectronic devices.

S. K. designed and supervised the research and provided funding resources. G. H. O. prepared blue-light emissive NCQDs and performed the PL, PLE, HRTEM, XPS, Raman, and TRPL measurement. B. S. K. prepared cyan- and red-light emissive NCQDs and performed the PL, PLE, FT-IR, XPS, Raman, and TRPL measurements. Y. S. designed the research and provided funding resources. All authors prepared the manuscript. G. H. O. and B. S. K. contributed equally to this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (NRF-2021R1F1A1063451 and NRF-2021R1A4A3031992).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.154690.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible quantum dot light-emitting diodes with simultaneous high brightness and efficiency

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Shen, Q. Gao, Y. Zhang, Y. Lin, Q. Lin, Z. Li, et al., Visible quantum dot light-emitting diodes with simultaneous high brightness and efficiency, Nat. Photonics 13 (2019) 192-197, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41566-019-0364-z.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High quality synthesis of monodisperse zinc-blende CdSe and CdSe/ZnS nanocrystals with a phosphine-free method

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-power genuine ultraviolet light-emitting diodes based on colloidal nanocrystal quantum dots
